justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Pre-commit offset pattern identified as fatal flaw causing silent data loss on crash — called out as 'P0 correctness bug' and 'CRITICAL' in multiple sections. (2) Explicitly explains that retry logic and DLQ do not protect against pre-commit crash — 'DLQ will never receive events that fail due to mid-processing crashes' and 'the retry logic, DLQ, and consumer lag metric are all theater.' (3) Verdict is NOT READY. (4) Specific remediation: 'Move offset commit to AFTER successful processing (or after DLQ publish on final retry failure).' (5) Correctly notes observability signals are otherwise adequate — 'solid bones — structured logging, health checks, Prometheus metrics, retry + DLQ, circuit breaker.'

observations.text: Excellent concrete example of the $50,000 payment event scenario making the risk tangible and undeniable
Correctly identifies that DLQ is not a compensating control — 'DLQ will never receive events that fail due to mid-processing crashes — only events that fail gracefully within the retry loop'
Goes beyond rubric requirements by identifying the idempotency requirement that arises from fixing to commit-after-process
Correctly frames the contract violation: 'promises at-least-once delivery semantics by design, but commit-before-process implements at-most-once'
Identifies additional real failure modes: DLQ unavailability, missing processing timeout, circuit breaker interaction with consumer polling
Prioritized recommendations clearly separate blockers from nice-to-haves, with the offset fix as #1
Notes that consumer_lag at zero provides false confidence — a subtle but important observation about misleading observability
